steps.title: Identify the paradox the conclusion must resolve
steps.content:
The passage establishes a tension: marginal notes indicate that much of Cantares Mexicanos predates the Spanish invasion, yet some poems contain "inarguable references" to Spanish beliefs and customs. The word "Thus" signals that scholars' conclusion must logically resolve this contradiction.
  • Choice A — 'while its content largely predates the invasion, Cantares Mexicanos also contains additions made after the invasion.': Directly resolves the paradox: most poems are pre-invasion (matching the marginal notes) while post-invasion additions explain the Spanish references. ✓
  • Choice B — 'although those who compiled Cantares Mexicanos were fluent in Nahuatl, they had limited knowledge of the Spanish language.': Poor knowledge of Spanish would make Spanish references less likely, not explain their presence; this does not resolve the paradox. ✗
  • Choice C — 'before the invasion by Spain, the poets of the Aztec Empire borrowed from the literary traditions of other societies.': The passage mentions only Aztec and Spanish traditions; borrowing from unnamed others is unsupported and does not explain specifically Spanish customs. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the references to beliefs and customs in Spain should be attributed to a coincidental resemblance between the societies.': The passage calls the references "inarguable," explicitly ruling out coincidence. ✗

steps.title: Confirm choice A resolves both sides of the paradox
steps.content: Choice A accounts for the marginal-note evidence (content largely predates invasion) and the Spanish references (post-invasion additions) without contradicting anything in the passage. The word "largely" preserves the marginal-note claim while "additions made after the invasion" explains the Spanish content. That matches choice A — A is correct.
